Outline
20.1	An Overview of Hormone Action
20.2	Modular Nature of Signal Transduction Systems: G Protein-Coupled Signaling
20.3	Receptor Tyrosine Kinases and Insulin Signaling

20.5	Signal Transduction, Growth Control, and Cancer
20.1 Modular Nature of Signal Transduction Systems: An Overview of Hormone Action

Signal Transduction, Hormone Action, and Metabolic Coordination
Signal transduction is defined as the reception of an environmental stimulus by a cell, which leads to metabolic changes that adapt the cell to the stimulus
There are four major classes of agents in signal transduction; all act through receptors:
hormones (act at a distance)
growth factors (action is long-lasting)
neurotransmitters (secretion close to target cells)
pheromones (act upon cells in a different organism)

Hormones (1 of 3)
In vertebrates, hormones are small molecules secreted by endocrine glands, which travel through the bloodstream and bind to specific receptors on or in target cells
Figure 20.1 The major human endocrine glands and their central nervous system control centers.

Hormones (2 of 3)
Several hormones are involved in the coordination of carbohydrate metabolism:
epinephrine, secreted from the adrenal gland, promotes metabolic energy generation in muscle
Figure 20.1 The major human endocrine glands and their central nervous system control centers.

Hormones (3 of 3)
Several hormones are involved in the coordination of carbohydrate metabolism:
glucagon, secreted by the pancreas, acts upon liver to promote gluconeogenesis
insulin, secreted by the pancreas, promotes uptake of glucose into cells
Figure 20.1 The major human endocrine glands and their central nervous system control centers.

Hormone Classes and Their Receptors (1 of 2)
Chemically, hormones include
peptides or polypeptides (insulin, glucagon)
steroids (glucocorticoids and sex hormones)
amino acid derivatives (catecholamines such as epinephrine and thyroxine, derived from tyrosine)

Hormone Classes and Their Receptors (2 of 2)
Hormone receptors may be located in the plasma membrane or within the cell (nuclear receptors)
Three major classes of membrane-bound receptors
proteins that influence second messenger synthesis
ion channels
proteins with intrinsic enzyme activity

Hierarchical Nature of Hormone Control (1 of 2)
The pituitary is the first target for most hormones and is under hypothalmic control
Pituitary hormones then act on secondary targets
Figure 20.2 Hierarchical nature of hormone action in vertebrates.

Hierarchical Nature of Hormone Control (2 of 2)
Neural stimulation of the adrenal medulla releases epinephrine
Figure 20.2 Hierarchical nature of hormone action in vertebrates.
20.2 G Protein-Coupled Signaling

Receptors as Defined by Interactions with Drugs (1 of 4)
Before the structure of hormone receptors were known, tissues and cells had been categorized pharmacologically in terms of their response to epinephrine analogs

Receptors as Defined by Interactions with Drugs (2 of 4)
The epinephrine (adrenaline) analogs such as isoproterenol and propranolol were classified as acting either as:
agonists – agents that act similarly to epinephrine
antagonists – agents that block the action of epinephrine

Receptors as Defined by Interactions with Drugs (3 of 4)
Propranolol antagonizes
adrenergic receptors
(controls blood pressure)
Isoproterenol agonizes
adrenergic receptors
(used to treat asthma)

Receptors as Defined by Interactions with Drugs (4 of 4)
In general, agonists are substances that mimic the effects of a natural compound by binding and activating the same cellular receptor; antagonists are substances that counteract the effects of a natural compound by binding to the cellular receptor and blocking its action

Biological Actions of Some Adrenergic Receptors (1 of 4)
Adrenergic is based on adrenaline, an older term for epinephrine
Adrenergic receptors were originally thought to be adenylate cyclases
Table 20.1 some biological actions associated with adrenergic receptors

Biological Actions of Some Adrenergic Receptors (2 of 4)
Table 20.1 some biological actions associated with adrenergic receptors

Biological Actions of Some Adrenergic Receptors (3 of 4)
It is now known that hormones that act through second messengers involve a three-protein module:
Receptor
Transducer (G-protein)
Effector (adenylate cyclase or a related enzyme)
Table 20.1 some biological actions associated with adrenergic receptors

Biological Actions of Some Adrenergic Receptors (4 of 4)
Table 20.1 some biological actions associated with adrenergic receptors

G Proteins ( 1 of 3)
are heterotrimeric proteins (α, β, γ) that can bind G T P and G D P via the α subunit
The binding of extracellular hormone or agonist to a G P C R causes a conformational change that stimulates the receptor to interact with a G protein

G Proteins ( 2 of 3)
This interaction of G P C R with G protein stimulates an exchange of bound G D P to G T P in the α subunit of the G protein
When G proteins bind G T P,
some
activate adenylate
cyclase; others
inhibit
adenylate cyclase

G Proteins ( 3 of 3)
The α subunit possesses weak G T Pase activity, slowly converting G T P to G D P
The γ subunit is usually
prenylated, anchoring the protein
in the membrane, while the
subunit is palmitylated and
subunit is myristylated

G E F, the guanine nucleotide exchange factor assist with
the dissociation of the
subunits from the
subunit, and the G T P
binding to the
subunit,
which initiates the association with adenylate cyclase (A C)
Figure 20.5 The cycle of G protein dissociation and reassociation.

A C is active when complexed
with

G A P, the G T Pase-activating protein helps to turn on the
G T Pase activty of the
subunit, initiating the release from A C, thereby deactivating A C
Figure 20.5 The cycle of G protein dissociation and reassociation.

Cholera toxin modifies the
subunit of

The Cycle of G-Protein Association and Dissociation (4 of 4)
In the intestine, c A M P accumulates and promotes the uncon-trolled into
secretion of water and
causing severe diarrhea and dehydration
Figure 20.5 The cycle of G protein dissociation and reassociation.

Effector Protein Adenylate Cyclase (A C) (1 of 2)
G-proteins have several targets but A C is the target in adrenergic signaling
A C catalyzes the conversion of A T P to the second messenger c A M P and pyrophosphate
Active second c A M P is eventually converted to (nonactive) A M P by c A M P phosphodiesterase

Effector Protein Adenylate Cyclase (A C) (2 of 2)
A C consists of two transmembrane
domains
and two
homologous cytoplasmic domains
that come together
to form the active site
Figure 20.7 Crystal structure of an adenylate cyclase catalytic domain.

Crystal Structure of the A C Catalytic
Domains Bound the α Subunit of  G sub S
Figure 20.7 Crystal structure of an adenylate cyclase catalytic domain.

Nitric Oxide (N O) as a Second Messenger (1 of 2)
Actions of nitric oxide:
Regulation of neurotransmission
Stimulating defense to microbial infection
Vasodilation
N O acts by stimulating guanylate cyclase, which converts G T P to c G M P
Action of c G M P is terminated by c G M P phosphodiesterase, which converts c G M P to G M P

Nitric Oxide (N O) as a Second Messenger (2 of 2)
Viagra is a c G M P phosphodiesterase inhibitor, so its action sustains levels of c G M P needed to stimulate blood flow, essential to the male sexual response

Phosphatidylinositol 4,5-Bisphosphate (P I P sub 2 )
as a Source of Two Second Messengers (1 of 4)
In the phosphoinositide system, G protein activates phospholipase C (instead of adenylate cyclase)

Phosphatidylinositol  4,5-Bisphosphate (P I P sub 2 )
as a Source of Two Second Messengers (2 of 4)
Phospholipase C cleaves
into the second messengers
inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate
and sn-1,2-
diacylglycerol (D A G)

Messengers (3 of 4)
binds and opens
calcium channels, releasing
from its intracellular E R
stores

Phosphatidylinositol 4,5-Bisphosphate (P I P sub 2 )

Protein kinase C is
activated by D A G and

Signal Transduction Pathways Involving Phospho-Inositide Turnover
Figure 20.8 Signal transduction pathways involving phosphoinositide turnover.

Cellular Processes Controlled by Phosphoinositide

20.3 Receptor Tyrosine Kinases and Insulin Signaling

Characteristics of Receptor Tyrosine Kinases (R T K s)
R T K s possess a single membrane spanning domain, an extracellular ligand-binding domain and a cytoplasmic domain with tyrosine kinase activity
The insulin receptor is an R T K but is unique in that, it exists as a covalently-linked dimer even in the absence of ligand

General Structure of R T K s
Figure 20.9 Representative receptor tyrosine kinase families.

Activation of the Protein Kinase Domain of the Epidermal Growth Factor (E G F) Receptor
Once the agonist is bound, the monomeric R T K dimerizes and the kinase domain is autoactivated by phosphorylation. The activated R T K recruits intracellular signaling or adaptor proteins.
Figure 20.10 Activation of the protein tyrosine kinase of the E G F receptor.

Signal Pathways Involving the Insulin Receptor
Figure 20.11 Signaling pathways involving the insulin receptor, illustrative of R T K s. I N S, insulin.
20.4 Hormones and Gene Expression: Nuclear Receptors

Characteristics of Nuclear Receptors (1 of 2)
Hormonal effects occurring through G-proteins are short lived, while those occurring through nuclear receptors involve longer term changes related to growth or cell differentiation
Nuclear receptors bind steroid and related hormones, which can diffuse through the plasma membrane
Ligand-bound nuclear receptors act on D N A and affect gene transcription

Characteristics of Nuclear Receptors (2 of 2)
Nuclear receptors possess a zinc containing N-terminal D N A-binding domain and a C-terminal hormone-binding domain
Nuclear receptors bind to sites on the D N A called hormone response elements (H R E s)

Target Organs for Steroid and Thyroid Hormones and Major Proteins Whose Synthesis Is Affected (1of 2)
Table 20.3 Target organs for steroid and thyroid hormones and major proteins whose synthesis is affected

Target Organs for Steroid and Thyroid Hormones and Major Proteins Whose Synthesis Is Affected (2 of 2)
Table 20.3 Target organs for steroid and thyroid hormones and major proteins whose synthesis is affected
Synthesis of each indicated protein is increased by the hormone,
except for the two identified by

Conserved D N A-Binding Sequences of Steroid Receptors
E R = estrogen receptor, G R = glucocorticoid receptor, T R b = thyroid receptor, P R = progesterone receptor, VitD = vitamin D receptor, R A R = retinoic acid receptor.
Figure 20.12 The conserved D N A-binding domain in steroid receptors.

Structure of the D N A-Binding Domain of the Estrogen Receptor Bound to D N A
Dimeric estrogen receptor D N A binding domain bound to an H R E
Figure 20.13 Binding of the estrogen receptor to D N A, as inferred from solution N M R spectroscopy.

Steroid Hormone Receptors as Drug Targets (1 of 2)
Tamoxifen is an estrogen receptor antagonist (it binds estrogen receptor but prevents the response)
The growth of some breast tumor cells is activated by estrogen; tamoxifen slows cell growth in these tissues

Steroid Hormone Receptors as Drug Targets (2 of 2)
R U 486 is a progesterone receptor antagonist and prevents the events necessary for a fertilized ovum to implant in the uterus
20.5 Signal Transduction, Growth Control, and Cancer

Signal Transduction and Cancer (1 of 2)
Most oncogenes (genes whose action is involved in carcinogenesis) are mutant forms of genes encoding growth factors or other agents in signal transduction
Table 20.4 Oncogene products as elements of signal transduction Pathways

Signal Transduction and Cancer (2 of 2)
Table 20.4 Oncogene products as elements of signal transduction Pathways
Source: Data from J.D. Watson, M. Gilman, J.Witkowski, and M. Zoller, Recombinat D N A , 2nd ed. (New York: scientific American Books,1992),p.339.

Conversion to Oncogenes
Proto-oncogenes can be converted to oncogenes by
infection by a virus or
mutation of the cellular proto-oncogene
Figure 20.14 Pathways by which proto-oncogenes can become oncogenes.

The Ras Oncogene Closely Resembles Normal Human Ras but Lacks G T Pase Activity
Ras protein is mutationally altered in many human tumors
Most mutations in ras are found in the switch regions which contact the G T P substrate
Figure 20.15 Structural basis for Ras activation by G T P.

p53 Is a Tumor Suppression Gene (1 of 2)
Tumor suppressor genes, in their normal form, serve to suppress tumors
p53 is a D N A-binding protein that signals a cell with major D N A damage to undergo apoptosis (programmed cell death)
Loss of p53 function leads to tumorigenesis
Roughly half of all human tumors possess mutations in the p53 gene

p53 Is a Tumor Suppression Gene (2 of 2)
p53-D N A complex. The most often mutated residues are shown in yellow.
Figure 20.16 Structure of the p53–D N A complex.

Chromosomal Translocation in Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia (1 of 2)
In this chromosomal translocation event, portions of genes 9 and 22 are linked, fusing the parts of two oncogenes (b c r and c-a b l)
Gene fusion yields a novel protein tyrosine kinase that stimulates growth-promoting pathways

Chromosomal Translocation in Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia (2 of 2)
Gleevec™ is a specific inhibitor of this oncoprotein and a very effective treatment for this form of leukemia

Neurons and Synapses
Figure 20.19 Multiple synapses on the body of a single neuron.
Each neuron in the brain communicates with roughly 1000 other cells. The synapses may be either stimulatory or inhibitory

Release of Neurotransmitters by Synaptic Transmission Events
Synapses use different neurotransmitters. For example, cholinergic synapses use acetylcholine, while dopaminergic synapses use dopamine
Figure 20.17 Transmission of a neural impulse across a synapse, such as a cholinergic synapse.

The Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor Is a Gated Ion Channel (1 of 3)
The nicotinc acetylchloline receptor undergoes an acetylcholine-dependent conformational change, opening the pore
Figure 20.18 The nicotinic acetylcholine receptor.

The Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor Is a Gated Ion Channel (2 of 3)
Acetylcholine is rapidly degraded in the synaptic cleft by acetylcholin-esterase, restoring resting potential

The Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor Is a Gated Ion Channel (3 of 3)
Nicotine is an agonist tubocurarine is an antagonist

Drug Abuse and Neurotransmitter Receptors
Many abused drugs act as agonists or antagonists of central nervous system neurotransmitter receptors

Prozac - A Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitor (1 of 2)
Secreted neurotransmitters have three fates:
Binding to postsynaptic receptors
Catabolism in the cleft
Reuptake into the cell for repacking into storage vesicles
Prozac selectively blocks serotonin reuptake, thereby increasing the amount that reaches the postsynaptic receptor

Prozac - A Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitor (2 of 2)
Although originally marketed as an antidepressant, Prozac is used to treat a wide range of psychiatric disorders

Chapter 20 Summary (1 of 4)
Hormones are regulatory substances produced in and released from cells in one organ, transported in tissue fluids (such as blood) and sensed by cells in other organs
Sensing hormones depends on the presence of specific receptors, which hormones associate with to initiate signal transduction pathways that lead to defined changes in metabolism or gene expression

Chapter 20 Summary (2 of 4)
G protein-coupled receptors (G P C R s) are integral membrane proteins
A hormone binds at a specific site of a G P C R located on the extracellular side of the plasma membrane, which triggers the interaction of a G protein on the cytosolic side of the membrane
This interaction activates the G protein, which then initiates the synthesis of second messengers (such as cyclic A M P) to affect target metabolic processes

Chapter 20 Summary (3 of 4)
Receptor tyrosine kinases (R T K s) are integral membrane proteins containing protein kinase domains on their cytosolic side
Upon interaction of a R T K with a hormone (such as insulin) on the extracellular side, the protein kinas activity of the R T K is turned on to act on processes at the gene and metabolic levels
Steroid hormones and related signaling agents act at the gene levels, through binding to receptors that control transcription of target genes

Chapter 20 Summary (4 of 4)
Neurotransmission is a form of signal transduction in which the signaling agent, a neurotransmitter, is released immediately adjacent to the target receptor
Many psychiatric drugs and drugs of abuse act as agonists or antagonists of neurotransmitters
